data.title: China's Looming Liquidity Trap Will Have Enormous Consequences
data.description:
NEW YORK (June 3) - We wrote recently about how the tariff war between the United States and China is unlikely to be resolved, notwithstanding the current "pause".

That's because the tariff dispute has less to do with trade and economics and more to do with geopolitical posturing between two adversarial powers: the incumbent power, the United States, and the rising power, China. We wrote about the mutual political risks from the trade war and the adverse political fortunes both nation's principals could face if the tariff situation deteriorated:

  • For Xi, layoffs and strikes leading to more widespread social disquiet in China. There are already widespread strikes in China, according to China's own government-controlled labor monitor.
  • For Trump, galloping inflation could cause a loss of GOP control of the House in the 2026 midterms to a Democrat House caucus, largely comprised of the same Representatives who had impeached him, twice, in his prior presidential term.

But the path for Xi and China seems to be more perilous: we foresee a liquidity trap leading to long-term economic stagnation, similar to that which was suffered by Japan in its "Lost Decade" from about 1991 to 2001.

In a liquidity trap, monetary policy becomes impotent because consumers tend to hoard money instead of spending it, even if credit rates are low. What's more, because rates are low, lenders tend to be less likely to lend and to hoard cash themselves rather than invest it. As consumption falls, prices fall in sellers' hopes of finding buyers. Theoretically, at least, a liquidity trap ends up in a long-term death spiral in which economic activity simply grinds to a halt: buyers stop buying; sellers stop selling.

China's looming liquidity trap is becoming more and more evident. The last two years, China inflation has not even achieved 0.25%. This chart of China's monthly Consumer Price Index for the last five years shows the CPI has turned negative - to deflation.

The World Bank estimates China's unemployment rate in 2024, before tariffs commenced, to be 4.6%. China's National Bureau of Statistics showed an unemployment print of 5.1% for April. Youth unemployment, for people 16 to 24, is substantially higher, with the South China Morning Post (SCMP) reporting a rate of 15.1% in April. The high rate of unemployment has created anxiety among many recent Chinese college graduates, who fear long delays in being able to commence their careers.

China's domestic producers are reportedly facing stiff challenges trying to sell Chinese products to the domestic Chinese market and are targeting the European market instead. But as we discussed in our discussion of the tariff pause, Europe will resist serving as the "shock absorber" of China's excess production.

In the United States, China's largest market, the SCMP reported exclusively that US retailers are pushing Chinese suppliers to shoulder up to two-thirds of the tariff costs, even as the retailers work to re-source production to other low-cost production centers outside China.

All these conditions virtually assure China's continued deflation and, thereafter, a liquidity trap. This prognostication is supported by two charts from the most recent Peoples' Bank of China survey of depositors, from 2024Q4, from before the President Trump imposed tariffs.

China's consumers have kept spending relatively stable, but a chart of Chinese depositors willingness to consume shows the money hoarders expected in a liquidity trap are emerging. The Chinese are saving more, investing less and consuming more, but mostly on things like education, healthcare, and tourism, not the consumer goods China needs to keep workers in its export goods manufacturing facilities employed.

China can and will attempt to counter the liquidity trap.

China has already launched fiscal stimulus to foster domestic consumption. But adding spending in so in a contracting economy is usually a fool's errand. It merely further weakens the currency.

A move beyond the zero bound?

In monetary policy, the Bank of China's e-CNY, China's Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC) is operational in at least 25 Chinese cities, as of early last month, according to GoldenPi, the Indian fixed-income investment site. The e-CNY is at par with the regular yuan, so its value cannot be controlled right now. But there is nothing to stop the PBOC from withdrawing physical currency from circulation and exchanging it for e-CNY to make it China's exclusive legal tender. (India did this with its two largest currency notes in 2016.) This would expedite China's plan to make the e-CNY its standard currency. Such a move would allow for the BOC to go below the zero lower bound to impose "negative interest rates" to counter the liquidity trap. This would cause the PBOC to engage in a systemic reduction in the value of the e-CNY held by the public to encourage the public to spend, rather than hoard cash. This would cause the signature consumer behavior of a liquidity trap - hoarding cash - to carry a cost.

But slashing the value of the e-CNY to impose an interest rate below the zero bound has obvious political consequences. It would slow - or even prevent - adoption of the e-CNY as an international payment platform, a long-term goal of the PRC. (In the USA, devaluation plus government surveillance of spending are the principal objections to a digital USD CBDC).

SUMMARY

In the short term, President Trump's China tariffs will raise the prices of Chinese goods for US consumers, but that will likely not last long; maybe until the end of 2025 or the first half of 2026.

In the meantime, China's deflationary trend will accelerate, causing the prices of Chinese goods to fall so precipitously relative to the prices American paid before the tariffs were applied. By the end of 2026, US consumers will barely notice a tariff has been applied.

In the meantime, China will suffer a slowly imploding economy, characterized by deflation, unemployment, and growing civil discord, especially among China's young people.

____________________________________

Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe to be under-performing. While we have never written about any companies that are our clients, and do not include companies we write about here in our personal investment portfolios, we do provide here strategies that we would have recommended were the companies our clients. Others discuss new management strategies of non-client companies we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions regarding such companies here, however, assume the company will not change).

Manager Commentary

As of 03/31/2025

“Lower Gross Domestic Product forecasts, coupled with declining consumer and business sentiment readings, reflect an economic backdrop that may start to show cracks under additional pressure.”

Income Research + Management

Market in Review

January saw an eventful start to the year with devastating wildfires in California, the emergence of China’s DeepSeek as an artificial intelligence (“AI”) competitor, and the beginning of President Trump’s second term, which jumped out of the gates with a wave of executive orders. Risk assets held in well through mid-February amid these developments, but intensifying tariff discussions and weaker economic growth projections drove stock prices down and bond spreads wider in the second half of the quarter. While inflation data suggested progress toward the U.S. Federal Reserve’s (“Fed”) 2% target — falling from 3% in January to 2.8% in February — market participants began anticipating a resurgence in inflation in the near future. The expectation of persistent price pressures was one driver of declining consumer sentiment, which dropped throughout the quarter to its lowest level since 2022. The Fed repeatedly highlighted the uncertain impact of tariffs on the economy, and it chose to keep interest rates at 4.25%–4.50% at both of its Federal Open Market Committee meetings during the quarter. The Fed’s updated dot plot indicated no change to its median forecast of two interest rate cuts in 2025. Front-end rates remained broadly unchanged during the quarter, while the belly of the curve saw yields decline, led by the 5-year tenor, which dropped by 0.43%. The curve inversion in the front end returned, with the spread between the 3-month and 5-year Treasury yields dropping from 0.05% to –0.35%.

Portfolio Performance

During the first quarter, the Harbor Core Bond Fund (Institutional Class, “Fund”) returned 2.70%, slightly underperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index, which returned 2.78%.

Within credit, our underweight to and security selection within the Industrials sector contributed to performance. The Fund’s security selection within commercial mortgage-backed securities (“CMBS”) and asset-backed securities (“ABS”) aided relative performance. The Fund’s out-of-benchmark allocation to Small Business Administration (“SBA”) loans was also additive to excess returns. An underweight to Treasuries was the largest detractor from excess return during the period.

Contributors and Detractors

The greatest contributors to performance included Eversource Energy (ES), Foundry JV Holdco, Penske (PAG), and SBAs. The largest detractors from Fund performance included GATX (GATX), Xcel Energy (XEL), a Ginnie Mae pool, Berkshire Hathaway Energy, and Columbia Pipeline Group. Despite the underperformance, we continue to be constructive on these holdings and will continue to hold these securities.

Positioning

We strive to remain duration neutral and curve neutral to the benchmark.

Positive contributors to relative performance included our security section within SBAs and the Industrials sector. The Fund's out-of-benchmark allocation to SBAs aided relative returns.

Relative performance was also helped by our overweights to CMBS and ABS. Detractors from relative performance included security selection within the agency residential mortgage-backed securities (“RMBS”) sector and an out-of-index allocation to RMBS.

The Fund's security selection within the A and AAA credit buckets, as well as our overweight to AAAs, aided relative performance. On the other hand, our underweight to AA and overweight to BAA securities detracted from relative performance.

We invest exclusively in U.S. dollar-denominated fixed-income securities.

Buys & Sells

During the quarter, we purchased BX Trust’s BX 2025 VLT6 Z in the primary market at a spread of 1.50%. This purchase screened attractive relative to peers. At this level, we believe that we are being compensated for the tenant risk in an otherwise very solid data center deal.

We purchased Global Atlantic 5.5% 2032 in the secondary market at a spread of 1.30%. This purchase screened attractive relative to peers. We also bought Textron 5.5% 2035 in the secondary market at a spread of 1.02%. This purchase screened attractive relative to peers.

During the quarter, we sold the SBA’s SBAP 2024-25F 1. This was an opportunistic sale due to relative value. We sold the SBA’s SBAP 2024-25G 1. This was an opportunistic sale due to relative value.

We also sold GATX Corp 6.05% 2054. This was an opportunistic sale due to relative value.

Sector Overweights and Underweights

Entering the first quarter of 2025, the Fund was underweight Treasuries and overweight securitized, a theme that held true throughout the quarter, as the Fund ended the period with a deepened Treasury underweight of 20.99% — an increase of 1.90% — and a securitized overweight of 15.69% — an increase of 3.11%. The securitized overweight grew mainly due to increasing our overweight to non-agency CMBS by 0.46% and increasing our overweight to MBS by 2.70%. Our relative exposure to credit stayed flat, ending the quarter with an overweight of 2.33%. Within credit, we decreased our overweights to the Finance subsector by 0.27% and to the Utilities sector by 0.20%, while increasing our underweight to non-corporates by 0.07% and decreasing our underweights to Industrials by 0.54%.

Country Allocation

The Fund's country allocation relative to the benchmark did not change during the quarter. IR+M invests exclusively in U.S. dollar-denominated fixed-income securities. With that said, consistent with our bottom-up approach, we will opportunistically purchase Yankee issues when we believe they are attractive on a relative value basis.

During the quarter, the Fund's absolute weight of Yankees increased moderately to 8.74%.

Outlook

Although tariff headlines drove markets during the first quarter, investors received little certainty on their final form and ultimate impact on the economy. The administration’s capricious approach drove up risk asset volatility in March, with Fed messaging bringing the word “transitory” back into vogue as it relates to potential, negative short-term economic impacts. Lower Gross Domestic Product forecasts, coupled with declining consumer and business sentiment readings, reflect an economic backdrop that may start to show cracks under additional pressure. Consequently, we maintain a cautious approach to adding incremental risk to our Funds and rely on our duration-neutral philosophy to help withstand periods of increased interest rate volatility.

Important Information

Risks

There is no guarantee that the investment objective of the Fund will be achieved. Fixed income investments are affected by interest rate changes and the creditworthiness of the issues held by the Fund. Credit quality breakdown is based on ratings from Moody’s, Standard and Poor’s and Fitch. In cases where all three credit rating agencies have assigned different credit ratings to the same security, the middle rating is used. In cases where the security is rated by two rating agencies, the lower rating is used and, in cases where only one rating agency has assigned a credit rating to a security, that rating is used. Securities that receive no rating from an independent agency have been categorized as ‘not rated.’ Certain unrated securities (such as derivatives) are not reflected in the data shown. U.S. Treasury and U.S. Agency securities appear under the category U.S. Government/Agency. The credit quality of securities in the Fund’s portfolio does not apply to the stability or safety of the Fund. The Fund itself has not been rated by an independent rating agency.

As a result of changing market conditions, total net asset levels, expenses and other statistics may change at any time and may differ from those shown.

The total amount shown for sector, industries, or country holdings may be greater than 100% because of the inclusion of derivatives and the collateral securities supporting those instruments. Sector allocations are determined using the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS), which is a service of Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor's (S&P).

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund before investing. To obtain a summary prospectus or prospectus for this and other information, visit harborcapital.com or call 800-422-1050. Read it carefully before investing.

All data for this attribution analysis is provided by Income Research + Management.

Linked Performance by Sectors data is produced from FactSet using data supplied by State Street Bank and Trust Company. Other is the contribution to relative return due to the cumulative differences in carry, rolldown, and convexity.

Shape is the contribution to relative return due to the difference between the portfolio’s and the index’s duration profiles. The basic formula is the [(difference of each key rate duration between the portfolio and the index) multiplied by the total return of the respective key rate Treasury] minus the Duration and Other effects.

Definitions

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Duration is a commonly used measure of the sensitivity of the price of a debt security, or the aggregate market value of a portfolio of debt securities, to change in interest rates. Securities with a longer duration are more sensitive to changes in interest rates and generally have more volatile prices than securities of comparable quality with a shorter duration.

data.title: Domino's Pizza: Avoid Due To Slowing Momentum And High Valuation
data.description:
Investment thesis

Domino's Pizza (NASDAQ:DPZ) shares are trading 17% below their all-time high reached at the end of 2021. However, the stock has staged a significant recovery since June 2023.

Further price gains are likely to depend on whether Domino's Pizza can continue its current expansion strategy in China and India.

Conversely, management has recently taken a more conservative stance on debt. Debt has actually been falling since 2022, which shows that management prefers to invest some of its cash in deleveraging rather than opening new stores.

I currently also expect top-line and bottom-line growth of at least mid-single digits for the next few years. However, with a P/E ratio of over 25, the stock is now quite expensive. The Q1 figures failed to fuel enthusiasm among investors.

Although management promises improvement in the course of the current fiscal year, I believe this is already clearly priced into the current valuation. In this respect, disproportionate growth potential is only likely if Domino's Pizza delivers better-than-expected figures. I consider this to be rather unlikely. I therefore only give the share a hold rating.

Growth momentum is slowing down

In the past, Domino's Pizza has continuously opened new stores. This strategy has enabled Domino's Pizza to triple its sales over the past 15 years while continuously improving profitability.

Management intends to continue this successful expansion model in the future, with a particular focus on developing countries such as China and India. However, Domino's Pizza's aggressive expansion strategy required it to take on new debt. This debt burden is an integral part of Domino's Pizza's financing strategy to support its planned growth. However, management has put the brakes on this in recent quarters and has even started to reduce debt. Management now aims to keep debt roughly constant while gradually reducing debt through profit increases. In Q1, the leverage ratio improved by 0.1x to 4.9x YoY.

Growth has slowed significantly recently. Although revenue still grew by 5.1% in FY 2024, Q4 2024 already saw a significant slowdown in momentum with growth of 2.9%. Q1 2025 was even lower, with revenue growth of 2.5%. The number of global net stores fell by 8 stores in Q1, which is also a clear sign that Domino's Pizza has moved slightly away from its former expansion strategy. In addition to expansion, Domino's Pizza is also focusing on closing unprofitable stores through franchisees, as in Australia:

Net stores were down by 25 in Q1 and this was primarily coming from closures from Domino's Pizza Enterprises, DPE, which is our master franchisee based out of Australia. DPE previously announced that they expected to close 200-plus underperforming stores, primarily in Japan, and substantially all of those closures took place in the quarter.

However, even international business, which had otherwise been so strong in the past, grew more slowly in Q1 2025 at 5% than in previous years (2022: 6.3%, 2023: 7.7%, and 2024: 6.5%). Here, too, we are seeing a slowdown in growth momentum.

The same store sales growth declined, particularly in the US market, indicating weakening consumer demand, especially in the US market. This consumer weakness was also confirmed several times by management in the earnings call.

On the bottom line, the company benefited from an 18.9% increase in net income, mainly due to a one-off effect. At the FY level, net income grew by 12.53% in FY 2024, already slower than in 2023 at 14.78%, and EPS was also lower in 2024 than in 2023.

We will see shortly that this continues a trend in which bottom and top-line growth will continue to level off in the single-digit percentage range.

Overall, we can therefore conclude that growth momentum is slowing, partly due to a slowdown in expansion, but also due to macroeconomic effects and declining consumer spending.

Looking forward

Management remains quite optimistic about the coming quarters.

First, we continue to expect our US comp to be 3%, and that it will be lower in the first half compared to the back half due to the timing of our initiatives. In the event that macro pressures persist, it could put pressure on achieving this number.

I am somewhat sceptical about top-line growth in US sales. Much hope seems to be pinned on the deal announced in April 2025 with DoorDash (DASH), which will allow customers in the US and later Canada to order Domino's Pizza directly via the DoorDash app:

We recently announced a partnership with DoorDash, the largest aggregator in the US. We began piloting in a small number of stores and are expecting to commence our national launch in May. We expect this rollout to be complete by the end of the second quarter with a meaningful impact from this new partnership anticipated in the back half of the year. So, in the second half of 2025 and beyond, we'll be competing on the biggest aggregator platform in the US, with one of the biggest pizza crust types in our industry, two things we could not have said only a few months ago.

While the partnership will undoubtedly expand Domino's Pizza's reach, it should be noted that DoorDash's contribution will be incremental. Only 50% of orders placed through DoorDash are expected to be genuine additional sales. Furthermore, this is nothing new, as Domino's Pizza has over 60 partnerships with aggregators. The question is, therefore, whether DoorDash will really be able to generate a significant contribution to Domino's Pizza's revenue in 2025.

Much of the international business also depends on the extent to which Domino's Pizza can execute its growth strategy in China and India. In China, the company plans to open at least 300 new stores per year from 2026 onwards, and in India, the target is over 200 net new stores per year. The current interest rate environment could give management a little more financial leeway to increase the leverage ratio.

In my view, however, the next few quarters will have to show that the current consumer climate in international business allows for high same-store sales growth rates. The slowdown in sales growth is a cause for concern. In the earnings call, management also only gave a target range of 1% to 2% for same-store sales growth in the international segment.

Second, we continue to expect 1% to 2% international same store sales growth as there continues to be macro and geopolitical pressures that exist around the globe and we believe this could impact our business.

The caveat regarding ‘macro and geopolitical pressures’ suggests that Domino's Pizza's targets are more likely to disappoint than to be overly modest.

Looking at analysts' estimates for the coming years, we see that revenue growth of 4.87% is expected for FY 2025 and 6.75% for 2026. On average, revenue is expected to grow by 5.8% between 2025 and 2030.

EPS is expected to grow by an average of 8.48% over the same period.

Both top and bottom line growth are thus below historical growth rates, which stood at 5.8% for revenue and 12% for EPS over the last five years. Even if we look at the individual years up to 2030, it is clear that Domino's Pizza will not achieve double-digit percentage growth in revenue or EPS in the coming years.

What investors get for their money

Looking at historical multiples, Domino's Pizza appears slightly undervalued despite its recent share price performance. The median 5-year P/E ratio indicates a theoretical upside potential of 10%.

However, as we are seeing a deterioration in growth momentum, the historical multiple is no longer justified. I do not find a P/E ratio of over 20 particularly attractive for a company with high leverage, single-digit growth rates and exposure to weak consumer sentiment.

The dividend yield of 1.5 per cent does not help either. Yes, this is a comparatively high figure for Domino's Pizza, as the yield has tended to hover around the 1 per cent mark in recent years. In addition, Domino's Pizza shares are an attractive dividend growth stock. Dividend growth has been 17 per cent and 19 per cent in the last five and ten years, respectively. The last increase in February 2025 was slightly below average at 15.2%, but the increase in 2024 was particularly high at 24.8%. However, this yield is not a reason for me to buy, especially as it is offset by a poor risk/reward ratio in terms of share price performance.

Conclusion

Overall, Domino's Pizza remains only a hold for me. The company has a proven business model, a clear expansion strategy in emerging markets, and an impressive dividend history, which are convincing arguments in its favour in the long term.

At the same time, however, we see a clear slowdown in growth momentum. The recent decline in same-store sales growth, particularly in the US market, and the management's cautious outlook point to an increasingly challenging environment.

In addition, with a P/E ratio of over 25, the stock no longer appears cheap. Only if Domino's Pizza delivers better-than-expected results in the coming quarters or the macroeconomic environment improves significantly could there be upside potential in the short term. However, I currently consider this to be unlikely.

Domino's Pizza is therefore only a hold.

data.title: Serabi Gold: Ore Sorting Leading To Free Cash Flow Generation
data.description:
Narrow Veins & Ore Sorting

For over 20 years, Serabi's (TSX:SBI:CA) economic value has been underpinned by the 100% owned Palito Complex, located in the State of Pará, Brazil. Palito is a high-grade underground mine where gold mineralization occurs in narrow veins. Since mining operations restarted in 2014, Palito has delivered consistent gold production of between 30,000 and 40,000 ounces per year. Based on Serabi's most recent production guidance, Palito is expected to produce 45,500 ounces of gold in 2025, which would represent an increase of slightly over 20% compared to the 37,500 ounces produced in 2024.

In December 2017, Serabi announced the acquisition of the Coringa Project from Anfield Gold for a total cash consideration of $22 million. As illustrated on the map above, Coringa is located approximately 200 kilometres south of the Palito Complex. When the transaction with Anfield was announced, Palito and Coringa were primarily connected by a dirt and gravel road. However, to Serabi's net benefit, the Brazilian government decided in 2019 to pave this stretch of the BR-163 highway.

According to the Feasibility Study published by Anfield in July 2017, the initial capital costs to develop the Coringa Project were estimated at $28.8 million. Considering Serabi's market capitalization of ~$40 million at the time, finding sufficient capital at an attractive cost was a key issue before being able to move Coringa into production. However, the new paved road between Palito and Coringa opened options that would have been impossible before.

The picture below illustrates a mineralized vein within Coringa's deposit. The first element to note is the mineralization's narrow width. According to the Preliminary Economic Assessment published by Serabi in 2024, Conringa's resource estimate was based on a minimum mining thickness of 1.0 meters, meaning that the mineralized ore coming from veins narrower than 1.0 meters would inevitably get diluted by waste rock. Obviously, there is a practical limit of how narrow mining can be done, given the required people and equipment.

The second element to note is the clear colour differences between the mineralized ore and the waste rock. Due to this unique characteristic, the average grade of the ore mined at Coringa can be increased mechanically with an ore sorter, which uses various optical properties to separate mineralized ore from waste rock. Of note, Serabi already had previous experience with this process, as ore sorting at the Palito Complex started in 2020. As indicated below, Serabi has been able to increase Palito's ore grade from an average of 1.86 g/t to 9.9 g/t after going through a Comex CXR-1000 ore sorter.

Introducing ore sorting at Palito created excess capacity at the mill, as less material had to be processed to obtain a roughly equivalent amount of gold. On the one hand, Serabi was dealing with excess mill capacity at Palito, and on the other hand, a paved road made Coringa easily accessible. Instead of building new infrastructure at Coringa, Serabi decided to install an ore sorter and to truck the high-grade ore to Palito's mill. In my view, it is a great way to extract value from Coringa while increasing the overall efficiency of Serabi's mill. Even if an entire processing plant is built later on at Coringa, the existing ore sorter will continue to be a valuable asset.

Positive Free Cash Flow & Net Cash Balance Sheet

For the full year of 2024, Serabi generated operating cash flows of $30.9 million. After accounting for $19.0 million in various capital and mine development expenditures, the company was left with approximately $12.0 million in free cash flow. With increased production volume and higher gold prices, Serabi generated free cash flows of $4.0 million in the first quarter of 2025, or the equivalent of $16.0 million on an annualized basis.

With healthy free cash flow generation over the last few quarters, Serabi is now sitting on $26.5 million in cash and cash equivalents, which easily covers the company's total debt of ~$5.5 million. With a net cash balance exceeding $20 million and annualized free cash flows of $16.0 million as of Q1-25, Serabi is doing pretty well from an operational standpoint.

In terms of absolute size, Serabi is indeed very small, but I think management has been doing a good job in deploying capital in an efficient manner with ore sorting at Coringa. Additionally, it enables management to gain a deeper understanding of Coringa's ore body before making larger investment decisions in the event of future discoveries. Compared to other junior producers I have researched so far, Serabi's management compensation also appears more reasonable than some of the multi-million-dollar packages I have seen. Unfortunately, share ownership is once again quite low among members of the management team, with the CEO, Michael Hodgson, owning only ~70,000 shares or ~$160,000 worth of stock as of December 2024.

After a ~160% increase in the share price over the last year, Serabi is now trading at a market capitalization of approximately $170 million. In this context, the annualized free cash flow of $16.0 million from the first quarter of 2025 would translate into a free cash flow yield of ~9.5%. On a theoretical basis, it means that Serabi would generate free cash flows roughly equivalent to its current market capitalization over a 10-year window, assuming the Q1-25 realized gold prices.

Even though valuation may seem attractive at first sight, Serabi is also running on the depletion treatmill. According to the 2024 Annual Report, Serabi ended the year with only 557,300 ounces of measured and indicated resources. On top, the Palito Complex ended 2024 with only 206,400 ounces of proven and probable reserves. Using Serabi's 2025 production guidance of 45,500 ounces at mid-point, we are talking about a reserve life index of 4.5 years when using 2P Reserves and a resource life index of 12.2 years when looking at M&I Resources.

I fully understand that with narrow and high-grade vein systems like the ones at Palito and Coringa, it would require a significant amount of drilling to build up a large 2P Reserve base. Moreover, Palito has demonstrated a solid ability to replace depleted ounces over the years, so management may find additional ways to expand mine life further.

Based on Serabi's current market capitalization and using Q1-25 annualized free cash flows, it would take approximately 10 years for an investor to recoup its initial investment if all free cash flows were redistributed. On the other hand, the company has M&I Resources equivalent to about 12 years' worth of production based on the 2025 guidance. In other words, once the investor in this example had recouped its initial capital, Serabi would have little resources left in the ground.

In this sense, are Serabi's free cash flows a return ON capital, or a return OF capital? Based on my personal experience investing in the gold mining industry, I think that returns ON capital are extremely rare in this business. In fact, an investor would have done pretty well in just keeping their capital intact over a 10-year window. Considering the incredible demand for fundamental surrounding gold, the industry as a whole may benefit from strong tailwinds. However, the fact remains that on a per-share basis, the gold mining industry may have one of the worst track records of shareholder value creation. After all, the depletion treadmill is real, and just replacing the ounces that you mine with reserves is already quite good in itself.

Risk Factors

Serabi's economic value is largely dependent on two small mines located in close proximity to each other in the state of Pará, Brazil. On top of geographical concentration, Serabi has only a few years' worth of mineral reserves relative to production. Even though the Palito mine has a long history of reserve replacement, it may not necessarily continue in the future. Finally, the company's two largest shareholders have sold a significant portion of their holdings in the last few weeks, and at least for me, it raises some questions. Do they know something that I don't?

Conclusion

Serabi is a junior gold producer operating the Palito and Coringa mines in the state of Pará, Brazil. With ore sorting at Ceringa, management has effectively unlocked value for shareholders while utilizing capital efficiently. Given its production outlook for 2025 and current gold prices, Serabi should generate healthy free cash flows once again this year. At one point, management will also have to decide what to do with the growing net cash balance. I guess it is kind of a nice problem to have, especially for a junior producer with restricted access to cheap capital. From an operational and capital allocation standpoint, Serabi's management has done a pretty good job in my view.

However, that does not mean that Serabi's stock is a buy. In fact, Serabi's two largest shareholders both sold a meaningful portion of their holdings in April 2025. According to the company's annual information form, Greenstone Resources recently sold 15.2 million shares, followed by Fratelli Investments selling an additional 11.7 million shares a few days later. Considering the recent run in the share price, I think they have done well for themselves, although it raises some questions for small investors like me. Why should I buy now if the two largest shareholders, owning more than 50% of the shares outstanding, have been selling? Do they know something I don't? If yes, how would I know?

Given its small size, why would Serabi remain a publicly listed entity, knowing that it could do pretty well for itself privately? In various interviews, Serabi's management also indicated their increased focus on marketing and on presenting the story to investors. I find the combination of increased marketing activities and selling from the two largest shareholders an interesting coincidence, to say the least.

I will conclude this article by saying that, from an asset quality and valuation perspective, Serabi is not what I am looking for. However, with my hold rating, I want to highlight management's efficient capital allocation, in addition to the company's healthy free cash flow generation and net cash balance sheet.

data.title: ULTY: Huge Risks For A Paltry Reward
data.description:
The YieldMax Ultra Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:ULTY) sounds like something bankers thought up in 2007 during the subprime mortgage crisis. But instead of packaging up subprime loans and selling them on, they are packaging highly volatile stocks and 3x leveraged ETFs into an "income" ETF and writing options on them in an attempt to boost an advertised yield of 145%.

It sounds risky, and it is. To make matters worse, the rewards are paltry.

ULTY Overview

ULTY uses a popular covered call strategy on a portfolio of 15-30 high-volatility stocks and ETFs. This produces income from the premiums, but also limits gains on the investments. Many will now be familiar with this trade-off, as there are a lot of ETFs using this strategy. However, UTLY takes a slightly different approach as it selects its holdings specifically on implied volatility (IV) levels, leading to higher premiums.

As a result, ULTY's holdings are high-risk. This is the basic top 10, but the full holdings are more complicated as each stock of ETF also has options attached.

By the way, the large holding is "First American Government Obligations," is basically cash held as collateral.

ULTY employs a fairly complex and wide-ranging options strategy on its holdings. As well as selling calls to collect the premium, it can also buy calls and sell puts to gain a synthetic exposure to stocks or ETFs. This is a way of leveraging their assets.

To achieve a synthetic long exposure to an Underlying Security, the Fund will buy that Underlying Security’s call options and, simultaneously, sell that Underlying Security’s put options to try to replicate the price movements of owning that Underlying Security. The call options purchased by the Fund and the put options sold by the Fund will generally have onemonth to six-month terms and strike prices that are approximately equal to the then-current share price of the relevant Underlying Security at the time the options contracts are purchased and sold, respectively. The combination of the long call options and sold put options provides the Fund with investment exposure equal to approximately 100% of the relevant Underlying Securities for the duration of the applicable options exposure.

The fund can also use alternative options strategies such as selling credit spreads and selling diagonal call spreads. In total, the prospectus lists 6 strategies, and the fund managers clearly have a wide variety of tactics to employ. This introduces execution risks, and the fund literature warns,

There is no guarantee that the Fund’s investment strategy will be properly implemented, and an investor may lose some or all of its investment.

To be honest, this aspect does not worry me so much. The managers will be painfully aware how much risk is already present in the structure of the fund so their trades will be constructed to minimize potential losses.

I am more interested in how effective the strategies are. In theory, selling calls with high volatility levels should produce some good income. However, it's not an easy aspect to track as there is no benchmark to compare the performance to. Any income should show up in the 30-Day SEC Yield.

**The 30-Day SEC Yield represents net investment income earned by the Fund over the 30-day period ended on the date indicated by the Yield, expressed as an annual percentage rate based on the Fund’s share price at the end of the 30-day period. The unsubsidized 30-Day SEC Yield does not reflect any fee waivers/reimbursements/limits in effect.

However, the fund page shows a yield of 0.00%.

It seems the income from options is patchy and some months the distributions are 100% from return of capital, while in other months there is a good proportion from investment income. For instance, September 2024's distribution was 75% funded by ROC and 25% from investment income.

However, looking through the available 19a-1 notices for fiscal 2024, there is only one other month with income. Furthermore, the "cumulative distributions" from investment income do not add up and a large proportion of the end figure seems to be there from the start of the year.

The FY 2024 information does not suggest there was significant income.

Risks

ULTY's strategy is not high risk. Indeed, selling covered calls should lower volatility and provide some downside protection. However, the holdings make ULTY a very high-risk fund. It holds several leveraged ETFs, including the Direxion Daily Small Cap Bull 3X Shares ETF (TNA). This was up 4.77% yesterday and is clearly a volatile holding.

Leveraged ETFs introduce negative compounding which means that a leveraged drop would need an even larger gain to break even. The SEC has issued warnings about this and provides this useful example:

Here’s a hypothetical example:

  • Let’s say that on Day 1, an index starts with a value of $1000 and a leveraged ETF that seeks to double the return of the index starts at $1000. If the index drops by 100 points on Day 1, it has a 10 percent loss and a resulting value of $900. Assuming it achieved its stated objective, the leveraged ETF would therefore drop 20 percent on that day and have an ending value of $800.
  • On Day 2, if the index rises 10 percent, the index value increases to $990. For the ETF, its value for Day 2 would rise by 20 percent, which means the ETF would have a value of $960.
  • On both days, the leveraged ETF did exactly what it was supposed to do – it produced daily returns that were two times the daily index returns.
  • But let’s look at the results over the 2 day period: the index lost 1 percent (it fell from $1000 to $990) while the 2x leveraged ETF lost 4 percent (it fell from $1000 to $960). That means that over the two day period, the ETF's negative returns were 4 times as much as the two-day return of the index instead of 2 times the return.

The TNA charts shows this behaviour clearly and a 86% gain is needed to reach the late 2024 high.

I wonder if ULTY's options strategy could make the situation worse. It will cap TNA's large 3x gains, but leaves the potential for 3 x losses.

Due to ULTY’s investment strategy, ULTY’s indirect exposure to gains, if any, of the share price returns of the Underlying Securities is capped. However, ULTY is subject to all potential losses if the shares of the Underlying Securities decrease in value, which may not be offset by income received by ULTY.

ULTY should still make large gains if the underlying holdings rally. This is a high-risk, high-reward fund. The question is whether the risks are justified. When I look at the fund's 1.51% total return since inception, I can't say it is.

De-Risking

One approach to holding a risky fund like ULTY is to de-risk by withdrawing the large distributions and parking them somewhere safer. Once the distributions equal your initial investment, you have completely de-risked and can hold for future dividends without much concern.

This sounds appealing, but the numbers don't quite add up. If I bought last June at $15, I would have received $8.98 in distributions over the last 12 months. I still need $5.02 to break even, but the price has gone down significantly and I would need at least another year to make my $15 back, and that is if the price stays stable. Looking at the chart, I am not optimistic that it will.

The risk is that price falls another 50%. I would then need to hold another year to break even and by the time I eventually do, distributions could be $0.05 a week, or $2.6 a year. Remember, my cost price is $15, so $2.6 a year would be 17%. It's not a bad yield, but it would likely keep shrinking, and remember, to get to this stage, you would have had to sit through 2-3 years of no yield. I'm not sure this tactic is as attractive as it first seems.

Conclusions

ULTY is an interesting product. By selling calls on extremely volatile assets, it should produce significant income. However, I can't find much evidence of this investment income. Perhaps future results or 19a-1 notices will clarify the situation.

Without evidence of the income, we are left with a very high-risk underlying group of stocks selected purely for volatility. These might perform well during a broader market rally, but there are significant risks involved. Given the total return of 1.51% in around 15 months, the risks do not seem ti justify the rewards and I rate ULTY a "sell."

data.title: Arm Brings Compute Platform Designs To Automotive Market
data.description:
While people have been talking about the automotive market as the next big tech device opportunity for some time now, in truth, today’s cars haven’t really advanced as far as many people hoped. Sure, we’ve got bigger, nicer screens and some level of assisted driving features, but we still haven’t gotten to the era of smartphone-level impacts for most of the vehicles we’re now buying or driving.

A big part of the problem is that the automotive development process continues to be long and tedious, in large part because of how technology gets adopted into modern vehicle platforms. Traditionally, car makers would wait until a given chip was design was completed, then integrate individual elements into more complete designs for their various car platforms, and only then could they start writing software for those finished hardware designs.

Last year, Arm (NASDAQ:ARM) took a big step towards reducing the standard 3- or 4-year development cycles by introducing two important innovations. First, they unveiled a full range of new automotive grade processor and other chip IP (intellectual property) that can be used in automotive platforms. Second, they debuted new cloud-based simulation and virtualization platforms in conjunction with major EDA (Electronic Design Automation) software providers Cadence (CDNS), Synopsys (SNPS) and Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY), as well as Amazon’s (AMZN) AWS. These tools allow automakers and their partners to start writing software before final chips using Arm-based designs become available. The result is savings of up to two years on the automotive software development process, allowing significant time reductions in getting advanced capabilities to market. (It will be interesting to see how the recent export limitations on EDA software to China impact some of these developments for Chinese automakers.)

This year, the company finished the process by integrating the different individual automotive cores into more complete system designs that can reduce both hardware design and software creation phases. In fact, the new offerings allow those steps to occur simultaneously, shaving up to another 12 months off the typical car platform development time frame. Specifically, under the company’s new Zena brand for Arm-powered automotive silicon designs, the company unveiled an implementation of their Compute Subsystem (CSS) strategy for the vehicular market. As with other markets that Arm first brought CSS to - including datacenter, PC and smartphones - the idea is to integrate multiple components around Arm’s latest CPU, GPU and other core designs, thereby reducing the complexity, costs and time associated with designing these higher-level systems.

In the case of the Zena automotive options, CSS enables a variety of different system-on-chip (SOC) or “chiplet” designs that provide different combinations of CPUs, GPUs, ISPs (Image Signal Processors), security cores, I/O (Input/Output) components, inter-chip connectivity and more. Specifically, the new Zena provides support for a 16-core v9 Cortex-A720AE CPU, coherent chip-to-chip connections via the CMN S3AE, a real-time processing-capable safety island through the Cortex-R82AE, options to connect multiple Zena designs together through UCIe, and more.

The net result is multiple complete options from which to begin hardware designs and to which software can be written. The systems offer plenty of room for customization by individual semiconductor or automotive makers through the open hooks they enable to connect different combinations of AI accelerators, image processors, graphics processors, and much more. Plus, different designs can be used to enable different types of applications from enhanced user interactions to more advanced in-vehicle infotainment (IVI) systems to assisted and autonomous driving features.

In fact, Arm believes it can drive the creation of what they’re calling an AI-defined car, which builds on the current concept of software-defined vehicles (SDVs) and brings more advanced AI processing into the experience. Given how rapidly AI has been integrated into other computing platforms, that seems a logical move - and the safety-focused capabilities Arm has integrated into the core Zena system will likely prove to be even more important here.

On a practical level, another key benefit of Zena is design efficiency for car makers. Importantly, it enables savings of not only time, but also the resources necessary to create vehicle platforms. This, in turn, lets automakers and Tier 1 suppliers create more platforms with less people (and in less time). In fact, Arm believes Zena CSS platforms can reduce the development costs/resources by as much as 20%.

For car buyers like us, that translates directly into getting later generations of technology into new car designs instead of buying cars that typically had semiconductor capabilities that were typically 2-3 years behind smartphones and other digital devices. For carmakers, this speeds time to market and allows companies to reuse certain hardware and software efforts across multiple product lines - instead of having to create new chip system designs and software for each range of models. Given how critical - and brand-defining - the digital capabilities of cars have become, this makes these development advances extremely important.

For Arm, this latest announcement is strategically important because it helps both extend and tie together the company’s effort in moving beyond individual chip IP into more complete system designs. This work to move up the value chain with CSS obviously allows the company to charge more for their offerings, but more importantly, it increases the significance of Arm platforms across all the markets in which it participates, including now automotive. Given that the company has increased its shipments into the car market by greater than 300% over the last 5 years and supports 94% of global automakers, it also seems clearly aligned with market demands as well, making it a win-win proposition.

In fact, as important as CSS platforms have proven to be in other markets where Arm has introduced the concept, the automotive market is the one that will most likely see the biggest benefit of it. The notoriously slow development times for car technology have been a challenge for many automakers and their major suppliers, and the dramatic potential speed-up that Arm is bringing to them with Zena should prove to be a real difference maker.

Disclaimer: Some of the author's clients are vendors in the tech industry.

data.title: Credo: Quietly Becoming AI's Favorite Cable Guy - Buy Call
data.description:
Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (NASDAQ:CRDO), a big player in optical and high-speed connectivity, just bumped up the AI growth story for the year. Credo is now guiding for Q1 2026 revenue to hit $185.00M-$195.00M versus consensus of $162.00M after bringing in $170M in revenue this quarter, up 26% Q/Q and 180% Y/Y, and comfortably ahead of estimates for $159.6M for the quarter. The fiscal Q4 results pushed the stock as much as 25% higher during the day and closed almost 15% higher on impressive guidance, adding to Credo stock 28% rally over the past month.

The stock has remained in the red YTD, down roughly 11% prior to the report, getting dragged down by DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) news back in January and again on Trump’s reciprocal tariff day. We’re calling Buy on the stock in spite of the post-earnings run-up because we expect the growth runway for connectivity solutions will continue to expand from here on higher hyperscale demand for AI services. The reason that Credo’s growth story should continue from here is based on the fact that they don’t have all their eggs in one basket, i.e., revenues are not dependent on a single hyperscaler’s CapEx spend, instead it’s spread across three hyperscalers that each account for around or over 10% of revenue.

Vivek Arya from Bank of America poked around those numbers a bit more in the Q&A section of the call Monday, asking about customer concentration, considering Credo’s revenue was largely dependent on Amazon (AMZN). Management explained that Credo’s largest customer, Amazon, made up 61% of revenue, followed by its second and third largest customers at 12% and 11% of total sales. The second is Microsoft (MSFT), and the third is suspected to be Tesla (TSLA). We tend to get cautious around companies that have concentrated sales with one hyperscaler, but after management’s response to Arya, we’re not worried that’s the case for Credo. Management noted that:

We expect to continue our diversification throughout fiscal '26. In addition to these three customers, we do expect to have two additional hyperscalers in the second half of fiscal '26.

Credo’s leading customer is Amazon, followed by Microsoft. Both hyperscaler players use Credo’s ACEs (active electrical cable) for AI and cloud infrastructure, and both of these players have confirmed their CapEx for the year without any downward revisions, which is a positive for Credo. Microsoft is eyeing a 45% Y/Y increase in CapEx this year to $80B while Amazon is aiming for a 20% Y/Y higher CapEx to $100B. Now, Credo’s biggest customer (the 65% one) is Amazon, which is good news considering Amazon has the highest CapEx spend from the tier 1 bunch, which includes Google and Meta as well. The reason we expect growth to continue from here is because of Credo’s diversification strategy, which is already happening.

Last quarter, management said Amazon was expected internally to make up the same amount of revenue in Q4 as in Q3. This means that the hypergrowth we saw this quarter was on Amazon's strength, but more importantly, on heightened strength from the other hyperscaler customers, driving an estimated 19% of sequential growth for the quarter. Further confirming this is the fact that in Q3, Amazon contributed 86% of revenues, while that number was normalized to 61% this quarter, as shown below. We expect customer diversification to continue to push revenues in FY26. For FY26, management is eyeing 85% revenue growth to +$800M on the ramp of its ACE, optical, and retimer product lines.

Credo’s flex is low-power, high-performance connectivity solutions, and its latest release is that of its ultra-low-power Lark optical Digital Processor family, created to address the needs of next-gen AI applications. This includes two optical DSP products: Lark 800 and Lark 850. Cignal AI Analyst, Scott Wilkinson captured the importance of this within the current AI backdrop noting that:

As AI drives datacenter growth to unprecedented levels, power becomes increasingly important. Every watt dedicated to optical interconnect is a watt that can’t be used for GPU power…With over nine million 800GbE modules deployed in 2024, and more than 14 million forecasted for deployment in 20251, Credo’s Lark DSP can play a pivotal role by reducing a major source of interconnect power consumption inside the datacenter.

Management added to this on the call, saying:

We achieved our revenue growth targets, delivering 50 gig and 100 gig per lane optical DSPs to a broad base of optical module customers…[and] we secured a significant DSP win for a 800 gig transceiver, with initial deployments expected at the U.S. hyperscaler in fiscal '26.

CEO Bill Brennan is expecting Credo to see a similar trend in FY26 as it did for FY25, noting that:

Quarterly revenue nearly tripled from Q1 to Q4, validating our foresight and our ability to capitalize on a predicted inflection point.

Valuation

Credo isn’t cheap, but we don’t think it’s supposed to be cheap considering it is a growth stock. The stock trades higher than the peer group average on most metrics; the stock trades at a forward EV/Sales of 14.62x versus peer group average of 2.79x and Forward Price/Sales sits at 15.22x versus a group average of 2.83x. The reality is that this is an expensive stock, but when pitched next to its more direct competitors like Broadcom (AVGO), it looks pretty cheap. Broadcom trades, for example, at forward EV/Sales of 19.64x, comfortably higher than Credo’s multiple. Credo’s forward PEG also makes the stock look more attractive, sitting at 0.45x, which indicates an estimated 73% discount to the sector. We think investors should be looking at Credo as a growth pick.

Credo stock is reflecting the momentum, up almost 43% on the one-month chart, outperforming Broadcom and Marvell (MRVL) comfortably, as shown below. Between the three, we're bullish on Broadcom and Credo and less positive on Marvell. RSI now sits in overbought territory at 76 after earnings yesterday, so we don’t think investors should jump in until we see that level normalized.

Bottom Line: Design wins will push growth

We think Credo will be an outperformer this year; the market understands Credo’s potential more after the recent earnings call, but we still see room for the stock to run come 2H25, considering new potential customers being added to the mix, including rumors about Apple (AAPL) being on the list. The industry is a highly competitive one, but we think Credo is carving out a permanent place for itself within the market that should grow alongside the appetite for AI. The company’s ability to do this is on the back of its design wins.

ACEs outperform traditional Direct Attached Copper or DAC and Active Optical Cables or AOC in terms of efficiency and power requirements for AI data centers; here are a couple of ways Credo’s designs will capture more market share. 1. ACEs save 50% power compared to the traditional default, 2. They use thinner copper that allows for reduced costs through lower cabling volume by up to 75% compared to older models, 3. They’re easy to deploy using a drop-on replacement without need for higher complexity configuration, and 4. They use integrated DSPs that boost data rates over longer distances without compromising on quality. We’re bullish on Credo and expect higher revenue into 2HFY26.

data.title: MCHI Has One Thing U.S. Stocks Don't: The Risk Premium
data.description:
Would you like to hear a statement that’s hard to accept, but justified by the math behind U.S. and Chinese market data?

Potentially, the probability has increased that the Chinese equity market, represented by the iShares MSCI China ETF (NASDAQ:MCHI), could deliver higher average annual returns than the U.S. market, represented by the MSCI USA Index.

The reason? Not expected EPS, which are still forecasted to be higher in the U.S., but rather a unique divergence between the monetary (and fiscal) policies of the two countries, which is creating a significant gap between the ERP of the two markets.

And it’s with this question in mind that I want to open this follow-up on MCHI.But first, here’s a quick reminder of why I still see MCHI as my reference point for the Chinese market.

What You Missed by “Skipping” the Latest Updates

I’ve consistently maintained a bullish tone on MCHI, even during contrarian phases. The reasoning behind this view has been the expectation of a mean reversion in the S&P 500 and a rotation toward undervalued segments, China among them. I’ve approached this thesis using just two ETFs: one more expensive but competitive in terms of returns, MCHI, and another younger (with lower AUM) but clearly more attractive in terms of expense ratio: FLCH. The comparison table on Seeking Alpha highlights these features.

I consider MCHI a potential core choice, thanks to its larger AUM; it’s objectively a more established solution in the market, with higher costs but solid, aligned returns. FLCH is still relatively young, but in my opinion, it can be interesting for those who see China as a satellite allocation.

Technically speaking? The performance is comparable, and there’s no significant difference in concentration: MCHI has fewer holdings, but also less concentration in the top 10; FLCH is the opposite.

If you’re looking for a more detailed analysis, I recommend checking out my latest update.

China vs. U.S. Valuation Analysis

According to the factsheet shared by MSCI, the forward P/E of the Chinese equity index is 10.82x. Since the current P/E is 12.80x, this implies an expected EPS growth rate of 18.3%. Taking the inverse of the P/E gives the earnings yield, which is 7.81%. From this, if the reported Chinese risk-free rate is 1.88%, the equity risk premium remains positive, almost 6% (around 5.93%).

RF (%) Earning yield (%) ERP (%)
1.88 7.81 5.93

Applying the same reasoning to the MSCI USA Index, with a P/E of 25.11x and a forward P/E of 20.53x, implies an expected EPS growth of 22%, which is potentially higher than China’s. So why all the concern? Because in relative terms, the U.S. clearly shows a negative risk premium. The earnings yield is 4%, and even assuming a conservative Treasury yield with virtually no swap spread, the risk premium would still be around -0.3%.

RF (%) EY (%) ERP (%)
4 4.30 -0.3

It’s impossible not to notice that the gap between the equity risk premiums of the Chinese and U.S. markets is wider than usual.

A Divergence

If we look closely at the data, we quickly understand that the reason why investing in China is technically more attractive lies in the divergence between the monetary policies of the Fed and the PBOC. The Fed still holds rates at 4.25%-4.50%, while the PBOC, in an effort to stimulate the market, is maintaining a declining policy rate.

Since the ERP is defined as the difference between the earnings yield and the risk-free rate, this mathematically outlines a process that, when explained verbally, often sparks debate: the process of capital migration toward economies with expansionary monetary (and fiscal) policies.

It’s a theme I’ve also discussed here in relation to Europe, and one I believe is crucial to understanding the long-term direction of global capital markets.

The Risks?

When we think about risk today, tariffs still come to mind. But in my opinion, the real concern is not the restriction of U.S.-China trade relations. What’s more concerning is the uncertainty that Trump’s policies transmit into the capital markets.

China’s exports to the U.S. have decreased, but the total size of China’s exports hasn’t dropped. And that’s the key point: it means China is managing, quite effectively, to decouple from the U.S. on the trade side.

However, what China is unable to do, due to the deep integration of the 2 countries’ capital markets, is detach from U.S. financial risk. What do I mean? That on the left tail of the S&P 500 return distribution, the correlation between MCHI and SPY increases. So, the way I see it, China is not a hedge (or at least hasn’t been in the past) against sudden market shocks.

In technical terms: when the VIX or MOVE spike abruptly, China’s hedging function tends to weaken, ironically, just when you would need it most.

So when I talk about tariff-related uncertainty risk, I’m referring to the possibility of negative shocks that could heavily affect the S&P 500 due to U.S. policy, and that, statistically, would also impact MCHI.

Odd as it may sound, I’m not worried about trade risk, which I already considered secondary even before the tariffs.

Conclusion

I remain bullish on China and believe the appropriate rating for MCHI is BUY. I’ve tried to find a rationale rooted in the math behind valuation multiples, and the result points to a risk premium above 5% in China, while it’s negative in the U.S.

With Treasuries yielding this high, and China maintaining an expansionary fiscal and monetary stance, my initial hypothesis of a progressive capital rotation becomes, in my view, increasingly valid.

data.title: Sell Upstart Holdings: Hard To Deliver On Its Promises
data.description:
What is an Upstart?

Upstart Holdings…

  • …Is a relatively new company. It was founded in 2012.
  • …Makes unsecured personal loans as its core product. As the company says, “Personal loans are commonly used to help with debt consolidation, medical expenses, home improvement projects, student debt, a move, a wedding, or a vacation.” Upstart recently widened its scope to make auto, home equity and home mortgage loans.
  • …Is pushing hard to lead on applying AI to its loan underwriting and marketing decisions.
  • …Is not a bank, so it can’t finance these loans with consumer deposits. It funds some of the loans itself, but generally sells them to banks and other investors.

This sales pitch from Upstart’s web site further helps define the company:

This is a relatively deep dive into the Upstart story, touching on these topics:

  • The stock’s roller coaster ride
  • The company’s grand vision
  • Demand for personal loans
  • The competitive supply of personal loan lenders
  • Competitor AI stories
  • Understanding Upstart’s earnings
  • Valuation approaches

Upstart’s stock’s wild ride

Here is the stock’s history:

The stock went public at $20 on December 15, 2020. It then soared to nearly $400 a mere ten months later. Wow! Then over the next 16 months it collapsed to $13. What? Today it sits at $50. What’s going on?

The initial enthusiasm up to $400 was due at least partly Upstart showing rapid earnings growth right out of the box. The subsequent 97% price decline was driven by three years of large losses. The recovery of the stock to today’s $50 price reflects the recent recovery to flat results, as this chart shows:

The (overly) grand vision

I am constantly amazed at the grandiose claims being made by all sorts of companies, and Upstart is very definitely no exception. Here are some quotes from Upstart’s May 14 investor meeting.

  • Let’s start with the title of the meeting: “Upstart AI Day”. The company works hard to brand itself as “the AI company”. AI efforts are pretty much ubiquitous in the world today, but somehow Upstart wants us to believe that its efforts are unique.
  • “Upstart is building the foundation model for credit” [my underlining]. “The” model? No other lender has a useful credit analysis model?
  • “The market is unimaginably large. $25 trillion in credit originated globally each year.” Upstart is currently actively participating in a mere 1% of that market, and it has only a 2-3% share of that 1%. I guess my local Sage Deli is part of a $3 trillion U.S. food and beverage market.
  • “At least $1 trillion in revenue is available to the platform that originates and services that credit.” [my underlining] Once again, focus on the word “the” here. Upstart assumes that only one platform is getting that $1 trillion. I wonder whose platform they are thinking of?
  • “Unlimited potential”. Sure, Upstart has unlimited potential. But then so does the Sage Deli.

Finally, a hype list you should see in full:

Wow.

The personal loan demand story

What is the demand for Upstart’s core product, the personal loan? A Google search turned up a lot of data from credit reporting agency TransUnion.

We learn that there was $250 billion of outstanding personal loans as of year-end 2024. That is a small subset of the $1.3 trillion of outstanding credit card debt and $20 trillion of total household debt.

U.S. consumer credit for a long time has been a mature product. For example, since 2012, credit card debt grew at only 4% annually, less than the 5% annual growth for personal income. Total household debt grew by only 3% a year since ’12. This chart compares credit card and personal loan growth rates since 2019:

As you can see, the two products grew at similar cycles, impacted by COVID. The COVID checks allowed many borrowers to pay down their expensive unsecured credit. Once the checks were spent, they reborrowed. At present, the growth rates are back to more normal low single-digit rates.

Why did personal loan growth have a big spurt in ’22 and ’23? I believe because fintechs, lead by Upstart and SoFi, more aggressively marketed the product, almost certainly to riskier customers. As a result, TransUnion reported that the personal loan delinquency rate rose from 2.3% in Q2 ’21 to 4.1% by the end of ’22. In turn, the lenders pulled back their marketing and growth slowed. For example, Upstart’s ’23 loan originations were less than half ‘22’s.

Looking forward, I expect personal loan growth to average 3-5%, continuing to be in line with household income growth.

The personal loan supply story. No shortage of competition.

Here is TransUnion’s data on competition for personal loans:

Since fintechs are relatively new companies, it is safe to say that competition for personal loans has grown materially.

And here is a list from NerdWallet of its best-rated personal loan lenders:

SoFi Discover LightStream Lending Club Upgrade Best Egg Upstart

Further, credit card companies compete for that unsecured credit. Now you are talking about behemoths like Capital One, Chase, American Express, Citibank and Wells Fargo.

To grow faster than the 3-5% organic growth rate I suggested above, an individual lender has to take market share. To do that, it has one of two choices:

  • Lend to riskier borrowers than most peers are willing to do.
  • Price below average.

Both strategies are highly risky. A history of consumer lending over my years of experience – since about 1990 – is littered with bankruptcies of fast-growing consumer lender start-ups. Anyone remember Advanta? Mercury Finance? FirstPlus? NextCard? New Century?

How then can Upstart grow faster than 3-5% a year without taking material risk? By being smarter and more disciplined than its peers. And that is exactly what Upstart’s management claims that it is doing with a superior AI effort. But a claim is different from a fact. And a fact is that…

Upstart is not alone in using AI for credit analysis.

Here is what some of Upstart’s peers have said about their AI efforts:

Capital One. From the Nvidia website - “Prem Natarajan, head of AI at Capital One, joined the NVIDIA AI Podcast to discuss how his organization is building proprietary AI systems.”

JPMorgan Chase. From its web site – “The goal of our AI Research program is to explore and advance cutting-edge research in the fields of AI and Machine Learning…Our global firm is committed to building partnerships and relationships with the best-in-class universities, students and researchers…”

SoFi. From its web site. “At SOFI AI, we are dedicated to revolutionizing customer interactions through intelligent automation.”

Best Egg. From an article co-authored with Amazon. “Best Egg achieved three times faster ML model training with Amazon SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning.”

American Express. From Forbes. “[American Express CIO] Radhakrishnan explained that American Express has been leveraging AI for over 15 years, particularly for predictive analytics in credit and fraud.”

Getting the idea? I conclude that:

  • Considering the far greater scale of competitors like Capital One, Chase and American Express, it is unlikely that Upstart has a material lead in the adoption of AI to credit analysis and marketing,
  • Smaller lenders will be able to access AI expert consultants, like Best Egg is doing with Amazon.
  • The huge global efforts to improve AI learning will make it more difficult over time for any company to have a financially valuable lead in data analysis, not just Upstart.

Understanding the accounting for Upstart’s earnings. Issue #1: “Normalized versus “GAAP”.

Seeking Alpha says that the Wall Street consensus for Upstart’s EPS is $1.48 this year and $2.19 next year. But it also says that Upstart’s Q1 estimates were $0.30 “normalized” and (-$0.03) “GAAP”.

The difference between “GAAP” and “normalized” is “stock-based compensation and certain payroll tax expenses”. In other words, Upstart has decided that employee pay in the form of share grants rather than cash is not an expense. Unless the shares have no value, I disagree. Yes, paying employees in stock rather than cash saves the owner cash flow today. But it reduces the owner’s future cash flow. Upstart went public with 73 million shares issued. Today it has 95 million. So the original shareholders have given up nearly 25% of Upstart’s future cash flow.

The accounting profession, through its Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB), agrees with me. And GAAP rules are the basis for P/E calculations for nearly all companies we value. So I’ll hold Upstart to the same standard.

Earnings issue #2: Gain on sale accounting.

The classic lending model has a bank holding the loans through payoff, financing the loans with bank deposits. The earnings generated by those loans are therefore recognized over the life of the loan. For example, a $100 loan may generate $2 in earnings per year for 5 years.

But Upstart is not a bank. It generally finances the loans it originates by selling them to other investors. It therefore records its profits at the point of the sale of the loan. In the above example, it may record an $8 profit, or the present value of $2 a year for 5 years.

But the present value calculation is complicated. Most importantly, few buyers of risky loans are willing to absorb all the credit risk; they want to ensure that the originator/seller retains skin in the game. So, as Upstart said in its last 10-K:

“In certain arrangements, the Company is obligated to make payments to these third-parties or is entitled to receive payments from them if credit performance of the loans sold...deviates from initial expectations…”

Upstart recognizes the deviations through a “fair value adjustment”. Do they ever occur? They sure do. To date as a public company, Upstart has recorded $423 million of fair value expenses, or roughly $5 per share. And the charges create a lot of earnings volatility, as this chart shows:

Finally, users of present value accounting can only grow earnings by growing loan originations. So growth past the natural consumer debt growth rate of 3-5% requires continual market share expansion. Which leads to those risks I described above.

Key earnings variables

Now that we understand the accounting, let’s identify the 6 key variables that drive Upstart’s EPS. Here is a summary of Upstart’s income statement:

(A) Origination volume. Upstart started out gangbusters, with $13 billion of originations. But despite its AI models, those loans proved unprofitable. As the company retooled, originations dropped by 64% by ’23. Now they are on the upswing. Considering the tough competition (I got personal loan solicitations from Amex and TD Bank over the past week) and expectations for a slower economy, how much will Upstart be able to continually grow originations?

(B) Origination margin. This number is all revenues (less fair value adjustments) divided by originations. There has been an almost perfect inverse relationship between originations and profit margin; a regression analysis says the inverse correlation is almost 100%! That is logical, as I noted above. So it is highly likely that Upstart will give up margin to increase business volume, which makes earnings growth much more challenging.

(C) Fair value adjustments. Remember that Upstart has a history of overstating its expected cash flow from originations, as its $423 million of historical loan value write-downs attests. A likely weaker economy, which will lead to higher loan defaults, increases the risk of future write-downs.

(D) Expenses. Upstart’s operating expenses have been relatively stable. If that continues, it will have operating leverage if it does manage to materially increase loan originations.

(E) Taxes. To date, Upstart has generated $414 million of losses. That’s the bad news. The good news is that it has created tax net operating loss carryforwards of about $350 million. It therefore won’t be paying taxes for many years.

(F) Shares outstanding. I said above that the significant amount of pay in company stock is adding to shares outstanding. Further, Upstart has over 10 million in stock options outstanding, which are added into diluted shares outstanding based on the share price. When the share price is high, like in ’21, most of the options are expected to be exercised. And when the share price is low, like in ’23, few options are expected to be exercised. I assume a 5% or so increase in diluted shares outstanding from a combination of both factors.

Forecasting Upstart’s GAAP EPS by adjusting Wall Street forecasts.

Seeking Alpha doesn’t report Upstart’s GAAP EPS forecasts, but rather the “normalized” numbers I argued against above. The company did give a GAAP estimate for this year: "GAAP Net Income to be positive in the second half of the year and positive for the full calendar year". Let’s give it $0.10 a share. For 2026, I’ll do some math:

  • The Wall Street consensus normalized EPS estimate is $2.19.
  • Upstart’s share grants were $34 million in Q1. Rounding up a bit, I’ll assume $150 million for the full year ’26.
  • Upstart’s diluted shares outstanding were 94 million is Q1. Let’s assume 100 million next year.
  • $150 million of share grants divided by 100 million shares out comes to $1.50 per share.
  • Subtracting off the impact of the share grants suggests a $0.70 GAAP EPS.

Valuing the stock – A P/E ratio approach.

At its current $50 price, Upstart is selling at 71 times my estimate of its ’26 GAAP operating EPS. The average for the S&P 500 is about 20. Peer consumer lenders Capital One and Ally Financial sell at P/Es of 10 and 6 respectively. Investors therefore assume that Upstart’s earnings will grow quite rapidly for a number of years.

Valuing the stock – A book value approach

For lenders, book value is a useful valuation measure. Upstart’s book value per share is $7.22. The stock is therefore selling at 6.9 times book. Capital One is selling at 1.2 times book. And more of Capital One’s value is not included in book value – the value of its bank deposits and any excess value in its loans. What explains the this substantial difference? Presumably investors’ belief in the value of Upstart’s AI capabilities. Using the excess of Upstart’s book value per share over Capital One’s as a measure, investors’ give Upstart’s AI skills a $4 billion value. We’ll see.

Conclusion – Don’t buy the stock.

It is just plain unlikely that earnings growth will be as fast as the current stock price assumes, for these reasons I’ve discussed above:

  • Slow growth in demand for Upstart’s core product, unsecured personal loans.
  • Tough competition.
  • The likelihood that AI capabilities will converge over time. I therefore expect that Upstart’s AI skills will prove to be worth far less than $4 billion.
  • The inverse correlation between loan originations and profit margins, which limits revenue growth.
  • A growing share count.
